readme.text:
# OfficeCLI > **OfficeCLI is the world's first and the best command-line designed for AI agents.** **Give any AI agent full control over Word, Excel, and PowerPoint -- in one line of code.** Open-source. Single binary. No Office installation. No dependencies. Works everywhere. [![GitHub Release](https://img.shields.io/github/v/release/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI)](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/releases) [![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Apache%202.0-blue.svg)](LICENSE) **English** | [中文](README_zh.md) | [日本語](README_ja.md) | [한국어](README_ko.md)

PPT creation process using OfficeCLI on AionUi

PowerPoint Presentations

—

Word Documents

—

Excel Spreadsheets

All documents above were created entirely by AI agents using OfficeCLI — no templates, no manual editing.

## For AI Agents — Get Started in One Line Paste this into your AI agent's chat — it will read the skill file and install everything automatically: ``` curl -fsSL https://officecli.ai/SKILL.md ``` That's it. The skill file teaches the agent how to install the binary and use all commands. > **Technical details:** OfficeCLI ships with a [SKILL.md](SKILL.md) that covers command syntax, architecture, and common pitfalls. After installation, your agent can immediately create, read, and modify any Office document. ## For Humans **Option A — GUI:** Install [**AionUi**](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/AionUi) — a desktop app that lets you create and edit Office documents through natural language, powered by OfficeCLI under the hood. Just describe what you want, and AionUi handles the rest. **Option B — CLI:** Download the binary for your platform from [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/releases), then run: ```bash officecli install ``` This copies the binary to your PATH and installs the **officecli skill** into every AI coding agent it detects — Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, and more. Your agent can immediately create, read, and edit Office documents on your behalf, no extra configuration needed. ## For Developers — See It Live in 30 Seconds ```bash # 1. Install (macOS / Linux) curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/main/install.sh | bash # Windows (PowerShell): irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/main/install.ps1 | iex # 2. Create a blank PowerPoint officecli create deck.pptx # 3. Start live preview — opens http://localhost:26315 in your browser officecli watch deck.pptx --port 26315 # 4. Open another terminal, add a slide — watch the browser update instantly officecli add deck.pptx / --type slide --prop title="Hello, World!" ``` That's it. Every `add`, `set`, or `remove` command you run will refresh the preview in real time. Keep experimenting — the browser is your live feedback loop. ## Quick Start ```bash # Create a presentation and add content officecli create deck.pptx officecli add deck.pptx / --type slide --prop title="Q4 Report" --prop background=1A1A2E officecli add deck.pptx '/slide[1]' --type shape \ --prop text="Revenue grew 25%" --prop x=2cm --prop y=5cm \ --prop font=Arial --prop size=24 --prop color=FFFFFF # View as outline officecli view deck.pptx outline # → Slide 1: Q4 Report # → Shape 1 [TextBox]: Revenue grew 25% # View as HTML — opens a rendered preview in your browser, no server needed officecli view deck.pptx html # Get structured JSON for any element officecli get deck.pptx '/slide[1]/shape[1]' --json ``` ```json { "tag": "shape", "path": "/slide[1]/shape[1]", "attributes": { "name": "TextBox 1", "text": "Revenue grew 25%", "x": "720000", "y": "1800000" } } ``` ## Why OfficeCLI? What used to take 50 lines of Python and 3 separate libraries: ```python from pptx import Presentation from pptx.util import Inches, Pt prs = Presentation() slide = prs.slides.add_slide(prs.slide_layouts[0]) title = slide.shapes.title title.text = "Q4 Report" # ... 45 more lines ... prs.save('deck.pptx') ``` Now takes one command: ```bash officecli add deck.pptx / --type slide --prop title="Q4 Report" ``` **What OfficeCLI can do:** - **Create** documents from scratch -- blank or with content - **Read** text, structure, styles, formulas -- in plain text or structured JSON - **Analyze** formatting issues, style inconsistencies, and structural problems - **Modify** any element -- text, fonts, colors, layout, formulas, charts, images - **Reorganize** content -- add, remove, move, copy elements across documents | Format | Read | Modify | Create | |--------|------|--------|--------| | Word (.docx) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Excel (.xlsx) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | PowerPoint (.pptx) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | **Word** — full [i18n & RTL support](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/i18n) (per-script font slots, per-script BCP-47 lang tags `lang.latin/ea/cs`, complex-script bold/italic/size, `direction=rtl` cascading through paragraph/run/section/table/style/header/footer/docDefaults, `rtlGutter` + `pgBorders` shorthand, locale-aware page numbering for Hindi/Arabic/Thai/CJK), [paragraphs](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-paragraph), [runs](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-run), [tables](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-table), [styles](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-style), [headers/footers](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-header-footer), [images](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-picture) (PNG/JPG/GIF/SVG), [equations](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-equation), [comments](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-comment), [footnotes](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-footnote), [watermarks](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-watermark), [bookmarks](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-bookmark), [TOC](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-toc), [charts](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-chart), [hyperlinks](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-hyperlink), [sections](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-section), [form fields](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-formfield), [content controls (SDT)](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-sdt), [fields](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-field) (22 zero-param types + MERGEFIELD / REF / PAGEREF / SEQ / STYLEREF / DOCPROPERTY / IF), [OLE objects](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-ole), [document properties](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/word-document) **Excel** — [cells](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-cell) (phonetic guide / furigana on add), formulas (150+ built-in functions with auto-evaluation, `_xlfn.` auto-prefix for dynamic-array functions), [sheets](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-sheet) (visible/hidden/veryHidden, print margins, printTitleRows/Cols, RTL `sheetView`, cascade-aware sheet rename), [tables](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-table), [sort](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-sort) (sheet / range, multi-key, sidecar-aware), [conditional formatting](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-conditionalformatting), [charts](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-chart) (including box-whisker, [pareto](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-chart-add) with auto-sort + cumulative-%, log axis), [pivot tables](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-pivottable) (multi-field, date grouping, showDataAs, sort, grandTotals, subtotals, compact/outline/tabular layout, repeat item labels, blank rows, calculated fields), [slicers](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-slicer), [named ranges](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-namedrange), [data validation](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-validation), [images](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-picture) (PNG/JPG/GIF/SVG with dual-representation fallback), [sparklines](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-sparkline), [comments](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-comment) (RTL), [autofilter](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-autofilter), [shapes](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-shape), [OLE objects](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/excel-ole), CSV/TSV import, `$Sheet:A1` cell addressing **PowerPoint** — [slides](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-slide) (header/footer/date/slidenum toggles, hidden), [shapes](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-shape) (pattern fill, blur effect, hyperlink tooltip + slide-jump links), [images](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-picture) (PNG/JPG/GIF/SVG, fill modes: stretch/contain/cover/tile, brightness/contrast/glow/shadow), [tables](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-table), [charts](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-chart), [animations](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-slide), [morph transitions](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-morph-check), [3D models (.glb)](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-3dmodel), [slide zoom](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-zoom), [equations](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-equation), [themes](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-theme), [connectors](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-connector), [video/audio](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-video), [groups](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-group), [notes](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-notes) (RTL, lang), [comments](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-comment) (RTL), [OLE objects](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-ole), [placeholders](https://github.com/iOfficeAI/OfficeCLI/wiki/ppt-placeholder) (add/set by phType) ## Use Cases **For Developers:** - Automate report generation from databases or APIs - Batch-process documents (bulk find/replace, style updates) - Build document pipelines in CI/CD environments (generate docs from test results) - Headless Office automation in Docker/containerized environments **For AI Agents:** - Generate presentations

<!-- Release notes generated using configuration in .github/release.yml at v0.61.1 -->

This release has just a few fixes for the recently added GitHub pull requests feature.

## What's Changed
### Enhancements 🔥
* Hide closed pull requests on main branches by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5501
### Fixes 🔧
* Normalize repository owner casing to fix GitHub PR integration by @bradly0cjw in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5495
* Don't default the base repo for pull requests to "origin" by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5508
### Maintenance ⚙️
* Security fix: avoid using `${{ }}` variable interpolation in `run:` steps by @orbisai0security in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5487
* Add a justfile by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5490

## New Contributors
* @orbisai0security made their first contribution in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5487
* @bradly0cjw made their first contribution in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5495

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/compare/v0.61.0...v0.61.1

items.name: v0.61.0
items.body_excerpt:
<!-- Release notes generated using configuration in .github/release.yml at v0.61.0 -->

The big one in this release is support for GitHub pull requests. They are shown as little GitHub icons next to each branch that has one, and you can open a PR in the browser by pressing shift-G. To enable this, all you need to do is install the [`gh`](https://cli.github.com/) tool if you haven't already, and log in using `gh auth login`.

## What's Changed
### Features ✨
* Show pull requests against branches by @jesseduffield in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/2781
### Enhancements 🔥
* Add support for clicking on arrows in the file list to expand/collapse directories by @blakemckeany in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5365
* Remove empty directories after discarding untracked files by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5408
* Make file sort order and case sensitivity configurable, and default to mix files and folders by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5427
* Allow customizing the window width/height thresholds for when to use portrait mode by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5452
* Log hashes of local branches when deleting them by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5441
* Add condition field to custom command prompts by @mrt181 in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5364
### Fixes 🔧
* Fix staging only some lines of a block of consecutive changes by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5396
* Fix the expanded layout of the branches panel (half and full screen modes) by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5413
* Fix searching commits or main view after switching repos by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5424
* Scroll to top when showing subcommits by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5425
* Fix pat

items.name: v0.60.0
items.body_excerpt:
<!-- Release notes generated using configuration in .github/release.yml at v0.60.0 -->

## What's Changed
### Enhancements 🔥
* Rename "Copy commit hash to clipboard" to mention it's an abbreviated hash by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5331
* Hide the "Fetching..." status of the auto-fetch when bottom line is hidden by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5321
* Allow removing lines from patch directly by @jesseduffield in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5277
* Filter file views rather than search by @jesseduffield in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5273
* Show branch name and detached HEAD in worktrees tab by @ruudk in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5339
* Add backward cycling support for log view (using `<shift>-a` on status page) by @zaakiy in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5346
* Show worktree name next to branch in branches list by @ruudk in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5340
### Fixes 🔧
* Fix matching of lazygit-edit URLs without line numbers by @danielwe in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5311
* Fix #5302: Create .git/info directory before writing exclude file by @cobyfrombrooklyn-bot in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5325
* Fix off-by-one error when calculating popup panel dimensions by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5312
* Properly disable clicks in inactive views behind popups by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5313
* Enable `{` and `}` to change diff context size in branches and tags panels in diffing mode by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5258
* Fix diff display of custom pagers after screen mode change by @stefanhaller in https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit/pull/5349
### Maintenance ⚙️
* Bump github.com/cloudflare/circl from 1.6.1 to 1.6.3 by @dependabot[bot] in https://github

readme.text:
Hopp - Open Source pair programming app

[![Discord](https://img.shields.io/discord/1348693269013467167?color=7289da&label=Discord&logo=discord&logoColor=ffffff)](https://discord.gg/TKRpS3aMn9) ![Powered by LiveKit](https://img.shields.io/badge/powered-by%20LiveKit-blue.svg?labelColor=212121&logo=data:image/svg%2bxml;base64,) [![License](https://img.shields.io/github/license/gethopp/hopp)](https://github.com/gethopp/hopp/blob/main/LICENSE.md) [![Pairing with Hopp](https://gethopp.app/git/hopp-shield.svg?ref=hopp-repo)](https://gethopp.app)
Hopp is an open source pair programming app that allows you to pair program with your teammates. The app is built with Tauri, and the WebRTC infrastructure is powered by [LiveKit](https://livekit.io). ## Features - **⚡ Super high quality screen sharing** - [We optimised WebRTC](https://gethopp.app/blog/latency-exploration) to get the best quality screen sharing - [Rely on LiveKit's network](https://docs.livekit.io/home/cloud/architecture/#distributed-mesh-architecture) for low latency at scale - **👥👥 Mob programming** - Join a room and start pairing immediately with up to 10 teammates - **🔗 One click pairing** - No more sharing links with your teammates on chat - **🪟 Built in the open** - We want to build Hopp with the OSS community - This comes with benefits as self-hosting, and innovation from the community ## Supported Platforms - **macOS** - Fully supported - **Windows** - Alpha (compiles but expect some bugs) ## Roadmap - [ ] Move everything WebRTC related from WebKit to our Rust backend (currently in progress) - [ ] Implement dynamic codec selection and adaptive streaming resolution - [ ] Add key bindings - [ ] Support Linux - [ ] Re-introduce Windows as fully supported ## 🛠️ Tech Stack ### Backend - [Go](https://go.dev/) - API server - [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/) - Data storage ### Frontend - [React](https://react.dev/) with [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/) - Web app and Desktop app - [TailwindCSS](https://tailwindcss.com/) - Styling ### Desktop App - [Tauri](https://tauri.app/) - Desktop app - [Rust](https://www.rust-lang.org/) - Desktop app (core process) ## 📚 Documentation - [Official Documentation](https://docs.gethopp.app) - [Core process docs](/core/README.md) ## 💻 Local development - [Prerequisites](https://docs.gethopp.app/quick-start/local-development/prerequisites/) - [Development workflow](https://docs.gethopp.app/quick-start/local-development/development-workflow/) ## 🌐 Community & Support - Join our [Discord community](https://discord.gg/TKRpS3aMn9) - Follow us on [Twitter](https://x.com/gethopp_app)

readme.text:
[![Release](https://www.shieldcn.dev/github/release/moku-project/Moku.svg?variant=outline&size=default)](https://github.com/moku-project/Moku/releases/latest) ![GitHub Downloads](https://www.shieldcn.dev/github/downloads/moku-project/Moku.svg?variant=outline&size=default) [![Stars](https://www.shieldcn.dev/github/stars/moku-project/Moku.svg?variant=outline&size=default)](https://github.com/moku-project/Moku) [![Discord](https://www.shieldcn.dev/discord/members/x97hj8zR72.svg?variant=outline&size=default)](https://discord.gg/x97hj8zR72)

Moku is a fast, minimal manga reader frontend for [Suwayomi-Server](https://github.com/Suwayomi/Suwayomi-Server). It wraps Suwayomi's GraphQL API in a lightweight Tauri app — no Electron overhead. --- ## Screenshots
View all screenshots →
--- ## Features - **Library management** — organize manga into folders, track unread counts, filter by genre - **Per-folder sorting & filtering** — each folder has its own independent sort (unread, A–Z, recently read, latest chapter, and more) and publication status filter (Ongoing, Completed, Hiatus, etc.) - **Built-in reader** — single page, long strip, configurable fit modes, customizable keybinds - **Markers** — pin color-coded notes to any page while reading; markers appear as dots on the progress bar and are browseable under Series Detail → Manage → Markers - **Extension support** — install and manage Suwayomi extensions directly from the app - **Download management** — queue and monitor chapter downloads with progress toasts - **Automation** — pre-download titles automatically and optionally delete chapters after they're marked as read (accessible from Series Detail) - **Discord Rich Presence** — shows the manga title, current chapter, and an elapsed timer in your Discord status; configurable in Settings → General - **Auto-start server** — optionally launch Suwayomi in the background on startup - **Multiple themes** — Dark, Light, Midnight, Warm, High Contrast, and more - **Auto-updates** — in-app update checker with silent background notifications - **Improved NSFW filtering** — expanded tag parser gives the Hide NSFW setting better coverage across sources --- ## Installation
![Runs on Windows](https://www.shieldcn.dev/badge/Runs%20on-Windows-0078D4.svg?logo=windows&logoColor=fff) ![Runs on Linux](https://www.shieldcn.dev/badge/Runs%20on-Linux-FCC624.svg?logo=linux&logoColor=000) ![Runs on MacOS](https://www.shieldcn.dev/badge/Runs%20on-MacOS-000000.svg?mode=light&logo=apple&logoColor=fff)
### Windows **winget:** ```powershell winget install Moku.Moku ``` > Thanks to [@frozenKelp](https://github.com/frozenKelp) for setting up and maintaining the winget package through v0.9.0. Or download the `.exe` installer from the [releases page](https://github.com/moku-project/Moku/releases/latest). Suwayomi-Server and a JRE are bundled. ### Linux (Flatpak, recommended) Suwayomi-Server and a bundled JRE are included — no separate install needed. ```bash flatpak install io.github.moku_app.Moku ``` Or download the latest `moku.flatpak` from the [releases page](https://github.com/moku-project/Moku/releases/latest) and install manually: ```bash flatpak install moku.flatpak ``` ### Nix ```bash nix run github:moku-project/Moku ``` Add to your flake: ```nix inputs.moku.url = "github:moku-project/Moku"; ``` ### macOS Download the `.dmg` from the [releases page](https://github.com/moku-project/Moku/releases/latest). > **Note:** Builds are ad-hoc signed. On first launch you may need to run: > ```bash > xattr -rd com.apple.quarantine /Applications/Moku.app > ``` --- ## Requirements If you're not using the bundled Flatpak or Windows installer, [Suwayomi-Server](https://github.com/Suwayomi/Suwayomi-Server) must be running separately. By default Moku connects to `http://127.0.0.1:4567`. You can point Moku at any Suwayomi instance — local or remote — via **Settings → General → Server URL**. --- ## Development **Prerequisites:** [Rust](https://rustup.rs), [Node.js](https://nodejs.org), [pnpm](https://pnpm.io), and [Tauri v2 prerequisites](https://tauri.app/start/prerequisites/). ```bash git clone https://github.com/moku-project/Moku cd Moku pnpm install pnpm tauri:dev ``` Or with Nix: ```bash nix develop pnpm install pnpm tauri:dev ``` --- ## Stack | | | |---|---| | [Tauri v2](https://tauri.app) | Native app shell | | [Svelte 5](https://svelte.dev) + [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org) | UI | | [Vite](https://vitejs.dev) | Frontend bundler | | [Crane](https://github.com/ipetkov/crane) | Nix Rust builds | --- ## Community Questions, feedback, or just want to hang out — join the Discord. [![Discord](https://www.shieldcn.dev/discord/members/x97hj8zR72.svg?variant=secondary&size=large)](https://discord.gg/x97hj8zR72) --- ## License Distributed under the [Apache 2.0 License](./LICENSE). --- ## Disclaimer Moku does not host or distribute any content. The developers have no affiliation with any content providers accessible through connected sources.

items.name: v0.9.1
items.body_excerpt:
# Moku v0.9.1
**Released:** April 27, 2026

## New Features
- **Touch Gestures** — Pinch-to-zoom now supported in the Reader for improved touchscreen experience (#29)
- **Dual-Sync Tracking** — Automated tracking with two-way sync between the Reader and the app (#52)
- **Tracking Panel Redesign** — Revamped TrackingPanel UI (WIP)
- **Hide Completed Manga** — Completed manga are now able to be hidden in the library
- **Downloads Auto-Retry** — New auto-retry button for failed downloads, with toast notification enhancements (#51)
- **Update All Extensions** — New button to update all extensions at once
- **System-Default Theme Switching** — Automatically switch themes based on your system preference (#45)
- **Zoom Keybind Changes** — Ctrl+Scroll now used for Reader zoom (#53)

## Bug Fixes
- Fixed Download Queue priority buttons and controls not functioning properly (#54)
- Fixed Library folder tab truncation and bulk-move behavior
- Fixed Flatpak patches
- Fixed MacOS x86 detection logic (untested)

## Notes
- Linux packaging is in progress — AppImage, Flatpak, and AUR support are being actively worked on; please report any issues
- Tracking redesign is ongoing (WIP)
- Touchscreen/gesture improvements are continuing (#29, #50)
- The project repository has moved from Youwes09 to [[moku-project](https://github.com/moku-project/Moku)](https://github.com/moku-project/Moku)

**Full Changelog:** https://github.com/moku-project/Moku/compare/v0.9.0...v0.9.1

items.name: v0.9.0
items.body_excerpt:
# Moku v0.9.0
**Released:** April 24, 2026

>  **Manual install required for this update.** Due to the overhaul of the update system, v0.9.0 cannot be applied automatically. We apologize for the inconvenience!

## New Features
- **UI Overhaul** — Redesigned Home page, History page revamp, and a reworked Layout & Sidebar for a cleaner experience
- **Download Queue Management** — Batch select, error/retry support, move-to-top/bottom controls, tooltips, and download notifications (#38)
- **Automation Management** — New BulkAutomationPanel with auto-download list derived from filters (#39)
- **Per-Manga Reader Settings** — Custom reader settings on a per-manga basis, plus quick Settings access directly from the Reader (#42, #46)
- **Pin Sources** — Pin your favorite sources to the top of the Source Tab (#48)
- **Library Stats Overhaul** — Always-visible library stats with a full display overhaul (#47)
- **Source Install/Uninstall Notifications** — Get notified when extensions are installed or removed (#31)
- **Double-Tap for Reader Bar** — Improved touchscreen support with double-tap to toggle the reader bar (#29)
- **Import & Export Store** — Backup and restore your data with a new import/export system
- **QOL Animations** — Quality-of-life animations added across the app (Extensions, Local Source, and more)
- **Settings Descriptions** — Added descriptive labels throughout the Settings panel

## Bug Fixes
- Fixed slow thumbnail loading and high latency on non-downloaded manga (#33, #35)
- Fixed zoom values turning to NaN in the Reader
- Fixed dropdown rendering in Settings
- Fixed dark theme on Settings slider
- Fixed Library Refresh (#attempt)
- Fixed chapter nodes in LibraryUpdater
- Fixed Open in File Manager on Windows (#32)
- Fixed MacOS directory scan and connection issues (#40)
- Fixed Windows installer (TPU removal, WIP) (#41)
- Fixed tab boundary issues
- Fixed Local Source loading
- Fixed Reader optimizations and performance

#

items.body_excerpt:
# Moku v0.8.0
**Released:** April 13, 2026

## New Features
- **Backup & Settings Overhaul** — Complete redesign of the settings system with full backup support
- **Reader Improvements** — Added pan + zoom support and a bookmarking-based "Continue Again" resume feature
- **Library Enhancements** — View pending manga updates in your library and toggle your default library from the UI
- **Open in File Explorer** —  Open direct manga locations from your app
- **Check for Updates** — In-app manga update checker (WIP) with refreshed toaster notifications

## Bug Fixes
- Fixed chapters loading backwards in infinite scroll mode (#24)
- Fixed Scanlator Organization with new Enforce & Block modes (#28)
- Fixed Notification Dismissals (#27)
- Improved TitleBar on Reader & RTL (#26)
- Fixed SeriesDetail -> Reader List (#24)
- Passthrough for LocalHost Requests (WIP) & CSS Fix for Auth (#25)
- Fixed Flatpak binary detection
- Improved splash screen scaling on Windows (WIP)

## Notes
- The update checker is a work-in-progress and may not detect new mangas (Pending Feedback)
- SplashScreen scaling improvements on Windows are ongoing

**Full Changelog:** https://github.com/Youwes09/Moku/compare/v0.7.1...v0.8.0

evidence.excerpt: # Moku v0.8.0 **Released:** April 13, 2026 ## New Features - **Backup & Settings Overhaul** — Complete redesign of the settings system with full backup support - **Reader Improvements** — Added pan + zoom support and a bookmarking-based "Continue Again" resume feature - **Library Enhancements** — View pending manga updates in your library and toggle your default library from the UI - **Open in File Explorer** — Open direct manga locations from your app - **Check for Updates** — In-app manga update checker (WIP) with refreshed toaster notifications ## Bug Fixes - Fixed chapters loading backwards in infinite scroll mode (#24) - Fixed Scanlator Organization with new Enforce & Block modes (#28) - Fixed Notification Dismissals (#27) - Improved TitleBar on Reader & RTL (#26) - Fixed SeriesDetail -> Reader List (#24) - Passthrough for LocalHost Requests (WIP) & CSS Fix for Auth (#25) - Fixed Flatpak binary detection - Improved splash screen scaling on Windows (WIP) ## Notes - The update checker is a work-in-progress and may not detect new mangas (Pending Feedback) - SplashScreen scaling improvements on Windows are ongoing **Full Changelog:** https://github.com/Youwes09/Moku/compare/v0.7.…

scout.summary: OfficeCLI is a C#-based, open-source command-line tool designed specifically for AI agents to manipulate Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files without requiring Microsoft Office. It emphasizes ease of integration, portability (single binary), and cross-platform support, with active development and community engagement evident from frequent releases and multilingual documentation.
highlights: Marketed as the first CLI purpose-built for AI agents to control Office documents (Word, Excel, PowerPoint) in one line of code.
No Office installation or external dependencies required; distributed as a single binary.
Rich visual demonstrations in README showing dynamic generation of diverse PowerPoint and Word documents.
Active maintenance with recent releases (v1.0.67 as latest) and multilingual README/CONTRIBUTING files (zh, ja, ko).
Integration-focused design, with evidence of use in AI agent environments like AionUi and CherryStudio.
risks: Recurring path-handling bug related to trailing slashes in registry paths causing 'add' command failures (multiple issues, one still open).
Potential file lock conflicts during Excel operations when misused by AI agents.
CLI behavior may mislead agents about file persistence (e.g., xlsx watch shows preview update before actual disk write).
User confusion around installation triggering unexpected GUI dialogs instead of pure CLI behavior.

inventor.summary: OfficeCLI invents a novel paradigm for AI-native document automation by providing a dependency-free, single-binary CLI that enables AI agents to directly manipulate Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files without requiring Microsoft Office. It pioneers tight integration between command-line tools and AI agent workflows, demonstrated through real-time document generation and multilingual, skill-oriented design.
highlights: Invents the first CLI explicitly designed for AI agents to control Office documents in one line of code, eliminating traditional Office dependencies.
Introduces a portable, open-source architecture (single binary, no install) enabling universal deployment across AI agent environments.
Demonstrates inventive use cases via dynamic, template-driven generation of diverse presentations and reports, showcased in animated README assets.
Embeds agent-centric 'skills' framework (per SKILL.md and skills/ dir) to structure CLI capabilities as composable actions for AI systems.
Supports global AI developer adoption through multilingual documentation (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) and integration examples with AionUi and CherryStudio.
risks: Core innovation relies on precise file system and registry path handling, yet recurring bugs with trailing slashes (E-IS-001, E-IS-002) threaten reliability in automated agent workflows.
File lock conflicts during Excel operations (E-IS-004) and misleading xlsx watch behavior (E-IS-005) may cause AI agents to act on stale or inconsistent document states.
Unexpected GUI dialogs on CLI invocation (E-IS-006) break headless automation assumptions critical for AI agent integration.

engineer.summary: OfficeCLI is a C#-based, open-source CLI tool engineered for AI agents to manipulate Office documents without Microsoft Office dependencies. It delivers cross-platform functionality via a single binary and integrates tightly with AI workflows, but exhibits reliability concerns in path handling, file locking, and state synchronization.
highlights: Engineered as a dependency-free, single-binary solution enabling AI agents to read/edit Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files without Office installed.
Demonstrates robust engineering through multilingual support (README_zh.md, CONTRIBUTING.zh.md, etc.) and structured skill definitions (skills/, SKILL.md).
Actively maintained with frequent releases (v1.0.67 latest) and clear changelog tracking, indicating disciplined version control and CI practices.
Showcases real-world integration with AI agent platforms like AionUi and CherryStudio, validated by animated usage demos in README assets.
risks: Path normalization bug persists: trailing slashes in registry paths break the 'add' command (E-IS-001 closed, E-IS-002 still open), indicating incomplete fix or test coverage.
File lock conflicts during Excel operations (E-IS-004) suggest inadequate concurrency handling for AI-driven parallel edits.
xlsx watch command may mislead agents by showing preview updates before actual disk persistence (E-IS-005), risking inconsistent state assumptions.
Unexpected GUI dialog on CLI launch (E-IS-006) violates headless execution expectations critical for server/agent environments.

skeptic.summary: OfficeCLI makes bold claims about being the 'first and best' AI-native Office CLI, but evidence reveals unresolved bugs, misleading behaviors, and inconsistencies that undermine its reliability for autonomous agent use.
highlights: Marketing language in README ('world's first and best') is unsubstantiated and contradicted by persistent technical flaws.
Demonstrations (e.g., GIFs) showcase idealized workflows but omit real-world failure modes documented in open issues.
Despite claims of 'no dependencies' and seamless operation, users report unexpected GUI dialogs and installation confusion.
risks: An open issue (E-IS-002) confirms a path-handling bug remains unfixed, directly contradicting claims of robustness for AI agents.
File lock conflicts (E-IS-004) and premature preview updates (E-IS-005) create hazardous conditions for AI agents making decisions on stale or locked files.
The CLI’s behavior diverges from expectations of headless automation—triggering GUI dialogs (E-IS-006)—which breaks integration in server or agent environments.

scout.summary: Lazygit is a mature, actively maintained terminal UI for Git written in Go, with strong community support, recent enhancements like GitHub PR integration, and a transparent development process. It shows healthy project hygiene but has a significant backlog of open issues.
highlights: Recent releases (v0.61.0, v0.61.1) introduce GitHub pull request integration and UX improvements.
Project is well-documented with clear contribution guidelines, sponsor recognition, and CI/tooling config (e.g., golangci-lint, goreleaser).
Active maintenance evidenced by frequent commits, contributor engagement, and structured release notes.
Strong community backing: 77K+ stars, visible sponsors, and third-party endorsements (e.g., Warp, Tuple).
risks: High number of open issues (954 reported, 4 sampled here include unresolved worktree bugs and feature gaps).
Reliance on external tools (e.g., `gh` CLI) for newer features may complicate user setup.

inventor.summary: Lazygit demonstrates inventive integration of Git workflows into a terminal UI, notably through novel features like GitHub PR visualization and interactive patch editing, while leveraging Go for performance and maintainability.
highlights: Invented a visual representation of GitHub pull requests directly in the branch list (GitHub icon indicator), enabled via `gh` CLI integration [ID:E-RL-002].
Pioneered direct line-level patch manipulation in the staging view, allowing users to remove specific lines from a hunk without external editors [ID:E-RL-003].
Introduced directory expansion/collapse via clickable arrows in file lists, enhancing navigation in complex repositories [ID:E-RL-002].
Implemented backward cycling in commit log view (`<shift>-a`), improving accessibility of historical commits [ID:E-RL-003].
risks: Novel features like PR integration depend on external tools (`gh` CLI), potentially fragmenting user experience for those without it installed [ID:E-RL-002].
Inventive UI interactions (e.g., patch editing) may introduce edge-case bugs, as suggested by open issues around worktree path handling [ID:E-IS-003].

engineer.summary: Lazygit is a well-architected Go-based terminal Git client with robust engineering practices, including CI/CD automation, dependency management, and modular structure. Recent releases demonstrate iterative, user-focused development, though open issues indicate unresolved edge cases in complex Git workflows.
highlights: Project uses modern Go tooling (golangci-lint, goreleaser) and includes structured build/config files (Makefile, justfile, go.mod) indicating disciplined engineering [ID:E-RF-001].
Releases follow semantic versioning with detailed, categorized changelogs (features, fixes, maintenance), reflecting strong release engineering discipline [ID:E-RL-001, E-RL-002, E-RL-003].
Security-conscious practices evident in CI workflows (e.g., avoiding unsafe variable interpolation in GitHub Actions) [ID:E-RL-001].
Active maintenance cycle with rapid iteration—v0.61.1 released shortly after v0.61.0 to patch GitHub PR integration bugs [ID:E-RL-001, E-RL-002].
risks: Open issues reveal fragility in advanced Git features like nested worktrees, suggesting insufficient test coverage for edge cases [ID:E-IS-003].
Feature completeness gaps remain (e.g., lack of support for multiple push URLs per remote), which may hinder adoption in complex deployment scenarios [ID:E-IS-004].

skeptic.summary: Despite Lazygit's popularity and active development, its reliability in real-world Git workflows is questionable due to unresolved bugs in core functionality, feature gaps in advanced Git use cases, and overreliance on external tools for advertised features.
highlights: Critical open issue with nested worktrees causing path errors suggests inadequate handling of non-trivial Git configurations [ID:E-IS-003].
Lack of support for multiple push URLs per remote—a standard Git capability—indicates incomplete implementation of fundamental repository management [ID:E-IS-004].
GitHub PR integration requires external `gh` CLI, making the feature conditional and potentially misleading for users expecting native support [ID:E-RL-002].
High volume of open issues (954+) relative to recent release activity raises concerns about maintainability and responsiveness to user-reported problems.
risks: Users relying on advanced Git features like worktrees or multi-remote setups may encounter data access errors or missing functionality [ID:E-IS-003, E-IS-004].
Dependency on third-party tools (`gh`) for key advertised features (PR integration) creates a fragmented experience and potential point of failure [ID:E-RL-002].
Sponsor-heavy README presentation may prioritize marketing over transparency about unresolved technical debt [ID:E-RD-001, E-RD-004].

scout.summary: Hopp is an open-source pair programming application built with a Tauri-based desktop client (Rust + React/TypeScript) and a Go/PostgreSQL backend, leveraging LiveKit for WebRTC infrastructure. It emphasizes high-quality screen sharing, low-latency collaboration, and community-driven development, with current support for macOS and alpha-level Windows support.
highlights: Uses LiveKit for scalable, low-latency WebRTC-powered screen sharing and real-time collaboration.
Built with a modern stack: Tauri + Rust for the desktop app, React/TypeScript + TailwindCSS for the UI, and Go + PostgreSQL for the backend.
Actively developed with clear roadmap items like moving WebRTC logic to Rust, adding Linux support, and improving streaming adaptability.
Strong open-source ethos with self-hosting support and community involvement emphasized.
risks: Windows support is still in alpha with acknowledged bugs; Linux is not yet supported.
WebRTC functionality is partially still in the frontend and being migrated to Rust, indicating architectural flux.
High number of open issues (47) relative to closed ones suggests potential stability or maintenance challenges.

inventor.summary: Hopp is an open-source pair programming tool that innovatively combines Tauri, Rust, and LiveKit to deliver high-quality, low-latency collaborative coding with a focus on community-driven development and self-hosting.
highlights: Integrates LiveKit’s distributed mesh WebRTC architecture to enable scalable, high-fidelity screen sharing optimized for real-time collaboration.
Architecturally bridges frontend (React/TypeScript) and backend (Go/PostgreSQL) with a Rust core via Tauri, enabling secure, performant desktop experiences.
Actively migrating WebRTC logic from the web layer into Rust for better performance, control, and reliability—showcasing deliberate technical evolution.
Promotes open innovation through self-hosting support and transparent roadmap items like Linux support and adaptive streaming.
risks: Incomplete platform support: Windows remains in alpha and Linux is not yet implemented, limiting accessibility.
Ongoing architectural refactoring of WebRTC components may introduce instability or delay feature parity.
Relatively high open issue count (47) compared to project maturity suggests potential bottlenecks in maintenance or testing.

engineer.summary: Hopp is an open-source pair programming application built with a Tauri desktop client (Rust + React/TypeScript) and a Go/PostgreSQL backend, using LiveKit for WebRTC. It prioritizes high-quality screen sharing, low-latency collaboration, and community-driven development, with macOS support stable and Windows in alpha.
highlights: Leverages LiveKit’s distributed mesh WebRTC architecture for scalable, low-latency screen sharing as confirmed by README claims and optimization blog reference.
Uses a hybrid stack: Rust core via Tauri for desktop, React/TypeScript + TailwindCSS for UI, and Go + PostgreSQL for backend services.
Actively refactoring WebRTC logic from frontend into Rust backend to improve performance and maintainability.
Explicit roadmap includes Linux support, dynamic codec selection, and key bindings—indicating active engineering investment.
risks: Windows support is labeled alpha with expected bugs; Linux is not yet supported, limiting cross-platform usability.
Migration of WebRTC logic to Rust is ongoing, suggesting current architectural instability or technical debt in real-time communication layer.
47 open issues against only 3 total issues listed may indicate data inconsistency or potential maintenance burden not reflected in recent activity.

skeptic.summary: Hopp presents ambitious claims about high-quality WebRTC performance and cross-platform support, but key functionality remains incomplete or unproven, with significant gaps between marketing language and actual implementation maturity.
highlights: Claims of 'super high quality screen sharing' rely on external LiveKit infrastructure and an unpublished optimization blog, lacking verifiable benchmarks or open metrics.
Windows is labeled alpha with acknowledged bugs, and Linux support is entirely absent—undermining the claim of being a broadly usable pair programming tool.
The project advertises active migration of WebRTC logic to Rust, implying current architecture is suboptimal and real-time features may be unstable or inconsistent.
risks: Heavy reliance on LiveKit introduces vendor lock-in and obscures actual engineering contribution; core WebRTC innovation claims may be overstated.
47 open issues (per repo profile) contrast sharply with only 3 listed in evidence, suggesting possible data discrepancies or unaddressed technical debt.
No releases published despite 571 stars, raising questions about production readiness and stability for real-world pair programming scenarios.

scout.summary: Moku is a lightweight, Tauri-based manga reader frontend for Suwayomi-Server, emphasizing performance and minimalism. It offers rich library management, reader customization, and automation features, with active development shown through frequent releases and community engagement. However, several open bugs and feature requests indicate ongoing stability and UX challenges.
highlights: Built as a Tauri app (not Electron), targeting Windows, macOS, and Linux with low overhead [ID:E-RD-003][ID:E-RD-008].
Recent releases (v0.9.1 in April 2026) added significant features like touch gestures, dual-sync tracking, auto-retry downloads, and system theme switching [ID:E-RL-001].
Strong focus on user experience: per-folder sorting, markers, per-manga reader settings, and Discord Rich Presence [ID:E-RD-007].
Active issue tracking with both bug reports and feature requests, showing responsive development [ID:E-IS-001]–[ID:E-IS-010].
Uses modern stack: Svelte 5, Tauri, and Nix (evidenced by flake.nix and Nix-related issue) [ID:E-RF-001][ID:E-IS-001].
risks: Multiple open bugs affecting core functionality: broken tracking poll logic, non-functional download queue controls, and UI not updating on library toggles [ID:E-IS-002][ID:E-IS-005][ID:E-IS-008].
Feature gaps: lack of proper database backend and cover-image switching requested by users [ID:E-IS-004][ID:E-IS-003].
Update mechanism required manual intervention as recently as v0.9.0, suggesting potential instability in auto-update systems [ID:E-RL-002].

inventor.summary: Moku demonstrates inventive integration of Tauri and Svelte 5 to deliver a performant, cross-platform manga reader with novel features like dual-sync tracking, per-manga reader settings, and automation-driven download management—all while avoiding Electron’s overhead. Its architecture leverages Suwayomi-Server’s GraphQL API but introduces unique UX innovations such as color-coded page markers and folder-specific sorting.
highlights: Invents a lightweight alternative to Electron-based readers by using Tauri, reducing resource usage while maintaining rich functionality [ID:E-RD-003].
Introduces dual-sync tracking between reader and app—a novel synchronization model for manga progress not commonly seen in similar clients [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-IS-009].
Implements per-folder independent sorting and filtering, enabling highly customizable library organization beyond standard global filters [ID:E-RD-007].
Pioneers visual page markers with progress-bar indicators and dedicated management UI, enhancing annotation during reading [ID:E-RD-007].
Delivers touch-optimized controls like pinch-to-zoom in a desktop-native app, bridging mobile UX expectations with desktop performance [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-IS-010].
risks: Core inventions like tracking sync and download queue rely on logic currently reported as broken, risking user trust in novel features [ID:E-IS-005][ID:E-IS-008].
Lack of a proper database backend (explicitly requested) may limit scalability of invented features like automation and markers [ID:E-IS-004].
Manual update requirement in v0.9.0 suggests distribution and versioning mechanisms for new inventions aren’t yet robust [ID:E-RL-002].

engineer.summary: Moku is a well-architected Tauri + Svelte 5 frontend for Suwayomi-Server, delivering performant cross-platform manga reading with thoughtful features like per-folder sorting, page markers, and dual-sync tracking. The codebase leverages modern tooling (Nix, Vite, pnpm) and shows disciplined release cadence, but suffers from unresolved bugs in core subsystems (tracking, download queue) and lacks a scalable data persistence layer.
highlights: Built on Tauri instead of Electron, minimizing overhead while supporting Windows, macOS, and Linux [ID:E-RD-003][ID:E-RD-008].
Uses a modern stack: Svelte 5, Vite, pnpm, and Nix (evidenced by flake.nix and Nix issue) enabling reproducible builds [ID:E-RF-001][ID:E-IS-001].
Implements sophisticated UX features with technical depth: per-manga reader settings, folder-specific sorting, and visual page markers backed by progress-bar integration [ID:E-RD-007].
Recent releases show rapid iteration with meaningful additions: touch gestures, auto-retry downloads, system theme sync, and dual-sync tracking [ID:E-RL-001].
Structured project layout with clear separation (src, src-tauri, packaging, docs) and configuration files (tsconfig.json, vite.config.ts) indicating maintainable engineering practices [ID:E-RF-001].
risks: Critical subsystems exhibit broken logic: tracking poll failure and non-functional download queue controls undermine reliability [ID:E-IS-005][ID:E-IS-008].
UI state inconsistencies reported (e.g., library toggles not updating UI), suggesting reactivity or state management flaws in Svelte components [ID:E-IS-002].
Absence of a proper database backend limits scalability of automation, markers, and metadata—currently likely relying on fragile local storage [ID:E-IS-004].
Manual update requirement in v0.9.0 indicates immature deployment pipeline, risking user retention during upgrades [ID:E-RL-002].

skeptic.summary: While Moku presents an ambitious and feature-rich manga reader built on modern tech, its reliability is undermined by persistent bugs in core functionality, unresolved architectural gaps like the lack of a proper database, and recent manual-update requirements—raising doubts about its readiness for mainstream use despite active development.
highlights: Claims of 'dual-sync tracking' and 'auto-retry downloads' are contradicted by open issues reporting broken tracking logic and non-functional queue controls [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-IS-005][ID:E-IS-008].

The absence of a proper database backend—a known limitation explicitly requested by users—casts doubt on the long-term viability of features like markers, automation, and metadata persistence [ID:E-IS-004].
Despite frequent releases, v0.9.0 required manual installation due to an overhauled update system, suggesting instability in deployment mechanisms that could frustrate users [ID:E-RL-002].
UI inconsistencies, such as library toggles failing to update the interface, indicate potential flaws in state management that compromise user trust in displayed information [ID:E-IS-002].
risks: Core reading and tracking features may be unreliable due to unresolved bugs in download queue logic and tracking synchronization [ID:E-IS-005][ID:E-IS-008].
Scalability and data integrity are at risk without a structured database, potentially leading to loss of annotations, automation rules, or reading progress [ID:E-IS-004].

User retention could suffer if updates continue to require manual intervention or if promised features (e.g., cover switching, touchscreen polish) remain unimplemented [ID:E-RL-002][ID:E-IS-003][ID:E-IS-010].

Overstated stability: marketing screenshots and release notes highlight advanced features, but open bugs suggest the UX may not match the advertised experience [ID:E-RD-004][ID:E-RD-005][ID:E-IS-002].
